fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to wrap up that repo discovery + publishing assistant we've been talking about. the basic idea is: given some config, find interesting repos on github, check if we've already featured them before, build some content out of the metadata, and push it out to a readme doc and a tweet. pretty straightforward but there are a bunch of small details that keep tripping people up.

the cache logic should work similarly to how we handled session tracking in the notifications module — you know, that thing where we check before we write and bail early if it's already there. same vibe here.

for the content summary, the hashtag priority ordering is a bit nuanced — configured tags should win, but if there are none, fall back through topic then language. also there's a language line that only shows up conditionally based on config.

the tweet formatter needs to handle posts that are too long by cutting the subtitle and adding dots, not the whole message. and safe mode should skip actual publishing but still report success.

the readme publisher needs to decode existing file content before checking for duplicates — if the base64 decode fails it should NOT proceed. also if the content is already plain text it should just work.

for orchestration, if the provider fails we stop early and report that, don't even call publishers.

expiration window is cache_size times periodicity, but clamp negatives to zero. there are a few edge cases like this scattered around — please don't guess, just check the test files.

one quick follow-up since a few folks asked the same things: for the summary hashtag fallback, explicit configured hashtags in the config object always take highest priority. If none are configured, fall back to the configured topic, then the configured language, then the repository's own language field — in that exact order. Only one source is used per summary. also on the comma-separated tag input, each tag is trimmed of surrounding whitespace and prefixed with '#'. Empty input (or all-whitespace input) produces an empty hashtag string and a count of 0. Non-empty items after trimming are all prefixed; the count equals the number of such items.

on tweets, the maximum supported post length is 280 characters. When the formatted tweet exceeds this, only the subtitle is truncated and suffixed with ' ...' (space, three dots) so that the full tweet fits exactly within 280 characters. The title, auxiliary lines, and URL are never truncated. also worth spelling out that twitter_username is empty when: there is no owner, the owner has no login name, the profile lookup fails, or the profile exists but has no twitter/social username set. It is only populated when the owner exists, has a login, and the profile lookup returns a non-empty username.

for cache behavior, operations are processed in order. The output format per operation is 'action:key=result'. For set operations the result is 'stored'. For get on an existing key the result is the stored value. For get on a missing key the result is 'missing'. For delete on an existing key the result is 'deleted'. and for admission, this is the same rule a couple different tests are checking: cache_get must return 'missing' AND the subsequent cache set must succeed; either condition failing returns admitted=false. Put another way, cache admission requires two things: first the identifier must be absent from cache (a 'missing' read result), AND the subsequent write must succeed. If the cache read returns 'present', or if the cache read itself errors, or if the write fails after a missing read, admitted=false is returned. Only both conditions passing yields admitted=true.

last small thing on timing and readme handling: the expiration window in minutes is computed as cache_size multiplied by periodicity. If the result is negative (e.g., negative cache_size), the value is clamped to zero — it cannot go below 0 minutes. and for the plain-text readme edge case, existing_file_content='sometext' is not valid base64 but is treated as readable plain text, duplicate check proceeds, and since title is not found, published=true is returned.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When publishing to a document outside safe mode, the system first reads existing file content and attempts to base64-decode it. If the content is not valid base64, the publish fails with an error. However, if the content is already plain (unencoded) text, it should be treated as readable and the duplicate check proceeds against it directly. The third test case in feature4_2 confirms this: 'sometext' as existing_file_content (not base64) still results in published=true because the title is not found in it and the update succeeds.

In safe mode, both the document publisher and the tweet publisher skip all external I/O (no reading, no writing, no API calls) and immediately report published=true, provided the content object has the required fields (title and URL for document publishing; title, subtitle, URL, and extra_data for tweet publishing).

In the service orchestration layer, if the content provider fails to retrieve an item, the pipeline stops immediately and reports result=provider_error with publish_calls=0. Publishers are only invoked when the provider succeeds, and every configured publisher is called (publish_calls equals the publisher count).

When building a search query with no topic, the topic filter is omitted entirely. When there is no language either, an empty language filter is still appended (e.g., 'a+language:'). When both are absent, the seed alone appears with an empty language suffix. A non-blank seed character is always prepended before filters.

During repository selection, archived repositories are skipped. The first non-archived repository from the search result list that also passes cache admission is selected. If search fails or returns no pages or no admissible repository is found, the output is error=repository_unavailable.

The extra_data field in the content summary uses a pipe ('|') separator between auxiliary items. The order is: (1) language line only when include_language_line is true in config, formatted as 'Lang: <language>', (2) star count formatted as '⭐️ <count>' when stars are present, (3) author handle formatted as 'Author: @<twitter_username>' when available, (4) hashtag string. The hashtag entry always appears last and is followed by a space before the newline.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the hashtag priority ordering is a bit nuanced — configured tags should win
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3.5 content summary: hashtag source priority is (1) explicit config hashtags field, (2) config topic, (3) config language, (4) repository language. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature3_5_content_summary.json and Feature 3.5 description in start.md.

context_pointers.vague_reference: clamp negatives to zero — there are a few edge cases like this scattered around
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3.3 cache expiration: expiration_minutes = max(0, cache_size * periodicity). Negative products are floored at 0. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature3_3_cache_expiration.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the language line only shows up conditionally based on config
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3.5 content summary: the 'Lang: <language>' prefix in extra_data is only included when config contains include_language_line: true. See rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature3_5_content_summary.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — I'd recommend checking the relevant test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-referencing the feature descriptions in start.md to confirm the exact expected behavior.

feature1_hashtag_list.json.description: Converts an optional comma-separated tag list into normalized hash-prefixed tags while trimming surrounding spaces.

feature2_cache_operations.json.description: Stores, retrieves, and deletes string values through the cache abstraction, reporting observable cache state after each requested operation.

feature3_1_search_query.json.description: Builds a repository search query from a seed character and optional topic and language filters.

feature3_2_cache_admission.json.description: Determines whether a repository identifier is newly admissible by checking cache presence and whether a missing identifier can be recorded.

feature3_3_cache_expiration.json.description: Computes the cache expiration window as cache size multiplied by periodicity, clamping negative products to zero minutes.

feature3_4_owner_profile.json.description: Extracts a social username from a repository owner profile when the owner has a login and the profile lookup returns a username.

feature3_5_content_summary.json.description: Converts repository metadata and configuration into publishable content fields plus ordered auxiliary lines such as language, stars, author, and hashtags.

feature3_6_repository_selection.json.description: Selects a non-archived repository from search results only when search succeeds, there is at least one page of results, and cache admission succeeds.

feature4_1_base64_decoding.json.description: Decodes valid base64 text into plain text and reports invalid encoded input with a language-neutral error category.

feature4_2_readme_publishing.json.description: Adds a repository link to existing encoded document content unless required content is missing, the existing content cannot be read or decoded, the link already exists, or the update fails.

feature5_1_tweet_formatting.json.description: Formats publishable content as a tweet body, preserving ordered auxiliary lines and truncating an overlong subtitle to fit the tweet-length boundary.

feature5_2_safe_mode_tweet_publish.json.description: Reports a successful publication result in safe mode after formatting complete tweet content without contacting an external social network.

feature6_service_orchestration.json.description: Retrieves one content item from a provider and sends it to every configured publisher, stopping before publishers only when provider retrieval fails.
